title: Old King Coal
original_prose:
"Now I been thinkin' 'bout the old days / I been thinkin' 'bout the times / And the way this world kept spinnin' / When I thought it might be fine"— Sturgill Simpson, "Old King Coal"



####

[INTERNAL FLAG — SYNRGY DATA FUSION NODE: REDACTED | CONFIDENTIAL ORIGIN: CORE OBSERVATION ARCHIVE]Entry: 4487-DeltaSubject: DEN-04 AnomalySummary: Broadcast signal detected on sub-basal frequencies. Cross-verified as non-SynRgy dialect. Presumed rebel scripture. Transmission encodes no payload but shows recursive waveform consistent with collective dreaming.Notes: Recommend immediate CORE cleansing.Status: Escalation pending.Remark: "There is joy in defiance. There must be no joy."



####



She sleeps like she forgot the world was ending.

Blanket tangled around one hip. Lips parted. A curl of ash still caught in her hair. The fire’s gone out but her skin’s warm. My chest aches looking at her. Not from love. From disbelief.

There’s no blood. No metal. Just Kat. Breathing.

I slip out from under the blanket. My boots wait by the stone. I don’t touch the sword.

The cave mouth glows faint. Morning light bleeding through mist and heat. I step out slow, trying not to wake her. Not yet.

Red Rocks is quieter at dawn. No guitar. No shouting. Just the sound of people trying not to break anything new.

I walk without direction. Past sleeping bodies curled in threadbare coats. Past prayer strings tied to a rusted mic stand. Past the remnants of last night’s stew still warm in a solar pot.

The murals catch my eye first. Charcoal. Blood. Burnt oil. Stories scrawled across the walls. Children’s handprints framing outlines of wolves and rebels and someone who looks like Kat with fire in her eyes.

I find the wall of names behind the old soundboard.

Hundreds. Maybe more. Carved into sandstone with knives or fingernails. Some clean. Some jagged. All the same size.

Not ranked.

Not sorted.

Just… remembered.

I run a hand across one. Feel the groove of it. The grit. Whoever it was, they mattered. Not because they fought. Not because they died. Because someone said they did.

The name isn’t familiar. Doesn’t matter. I flinch anyway.

Because I know what it means to be forgotten. To be filed instead of mourned.

Someone hums behind me. Low and broken. I turn, but no one’s there.

Just the wind, maybe.

Or maybe the grid remembering how to be human.

Either way—I’m not ready to go back inside yet.

Not ready to see if this morning will stay real.

Not ready to ask her what we’re supposed to do now that we’ve tasted something better than war.

I walk on.

Toward the old ridgeline, where the cliff gives way to morning. Toward the quiet.

Someone’s tending fire there. A boy no older than twelve, poking coals with a stick like it’s a wand. He doesn’t look up when I approach.

“Want some tea?” he asks, before I even speak.

I nod. He pours from a dented kettle into two cups that don’t match. One’s a SynRgy-branded steel mug, scratched to hell. The other’s bone china, floral, chipped near the lip.

I take the chipped one. Don’t ask why.

He doesn’t introduce himself. Doesn’t ask my name. Just sips and watches the canyon.

“They think you’re gonna break her heart,” he says eventually.

I choke a little. “What?”

He shrugs. “Kat. You know. The woman who sang the whole fuckin’ amphitheater to tears last night?”

“I know who she is.”

He nods. “Good. ’Cause some people forget. They start thinking she’s just one more rebel. One more voice. She’s not. She’s the song.”

I sip the tea. It’s bitter. Medicinal. Probably sage or something worse.

“You always talk this much?”

“Only when I don’t want to be afraid,” he says. “That’s why people talk. Didn’t they teach you that in whatever place you got all those scars?”

I glance down. Shirt pulled open a little. Burn marks. Knife work. Old WRAITH stamp across the ribs.

“No,” I say. “They taught us silence was strength.”

“Bullshit,” he says. “Silence is how you let bad things live too long.”

He tosses his tea into the coals and stands.

“Don’t wait too long,” he says. “She’s gonna wake up thinking you regret it.”

I don’t know what to say. So I don’t.

He vanishes into a side tunnel before I can ask who he is.

I finish the tea.

When I finally make it back, Kat’s sitting up. Blanket still over her knees. Bare shoulders catching the morning light. Her hair’s tied up now, loose and high. Her eyes track me before I speak.

“Good walk?”

I nod. “Ran into a prophet.”

She laughs once. “There’s a lot of those around here. Don’t feed ’em after dark.”

I sit beside her. She leans her head against my shoulder.

“I thought maybe you were gone,” she says.

I swallow. “I thought maybe I was, too.”

She doesn’t ask what I mean. Just pulls the blanket higher and takes my hand.

The sword’s still near the wall. Silent. Watching. But it doesn’t hum.

We sit like that for a while.

Then she speaks.

“Do you want this?”

I don’t answer right away. She doesn’t push.

“I want to want it,” I say finally.

“That’s a start.”

She stands. Grabs her boots. Tosses me mine.

“C’mon. You’re on breakfast duty. Ain’t no spectators in a free world.”

I catch the boots. Pull them on.

She walks ahead. Barefoot. Always.

And I follow.

Because for the first time, I don’t have orders.

Just breath.

And time.



####

Breakfast smells like smoke and rosemary.

The prep station’s half inside a crumbled sound booth, half spilling out into the open air. Tables made from old risers. Knives dulled to safety. Pots scavenged from twenty different decades. Everything’s mismatched and perfect.

Kat’s already in the middle of it. No throne. No orders. Just sleeves rolled up and hair tied high, stirring something thick and steaming in a pot the size of a bath.

She sees me hesitate.

“C’mon, Captain. You think the porridge stirs itself?”

I step forward. Slow. No one tells me where to go.

That’s the problem.

In SynRgy kitchens, every task had a number. Every number had a role. Order meant survival. Fuck up the timing on a starch drop and you lost protein allocation for a week. Here, no one's checking. No one's watching.

It makes me sweat more than combat.

“Here,” Kat says, handing me a bowl and a ladle. “Start there. That’s rehydrated lentils with something they swear ain’t rat.”

A girl of maybe six giggles at my expression. She’s barefoot. Hair in braids with copper wiring woven through. She grabs a chipped bowl and holds it out to me like I’m a priest.

I ladle it out. Slop hits the edge. She doesn’t mind. Just grins and says, “Thanks, mister!”

Then she’s gone, skipping back into a sea of arms and chatter.

“Was I that obvious?” I ask.

Kat leans in. “You look like you miss getting barked at.”

“Not exactly.”

“Sure you do. Structure’s comfy. Structure means you know where you stand.”

“And this?”

She gestures to the whole scene. Kids climbing scaffolds. Elders debating the best place to plant new growbags. Lovers stealing bites off each other’s plates.

“This,” she says, “is standing together. Whether you know your place or not.”

A boy about ten slides next to me, eyes wide. “You were the one who came with Kat, right?”

I nod.

He snickers. “You dropped your spoon in the pot when she winked at you.”

More laughter. At least four kids now. One even starts mimicking my stiff stance like a soldier at attention.

They’re not cruel. Just... free.

“Yeah, well,” I mutter. “I’ve seen worse things than lentils.”

“Bet you haven’t seen Echo porridge fight back,” Kat says, lifting the lid on a second pot. Steam rises like a ghost. The smell is aggressively herbal.

“I take it back,” I say. “I’m terrified.”

She bumps my hip with hers. Just enough to say, you’re doing fine.

I keep ladling.

The line moves. Hands reach out. Faces smile. No barcodes. No rations. Just people, serving and being served.

And I can’t stop the way my hands shake.

Not because I’m scared.

Because for the first time since Kansas, I want this to last.

And I don’t believe it will.



####

They call it the Circle, but it isn’t shaped like one. Not anymore.

Old greenroom walls turned to planter beds. Concert cables strung with drying herbs. A cracked mirror used as a light reflector.

The smell hits first—earth and eucalyptus, sage and sweat. Not clean. Not sterile. Just alive.

A woman with deep laugh lines and a melted synth-arm waves me in without looking up. She’s trimming something purple and spiny while humming what might be gospel or a glitch loop.

“You look like you ache in places language forgot,” she says.

I nod. “Everywhere.”

She jerks a thumb at a corner mat. “Sit. Breathe. Don’t talk unless you need to.”

A young boy kneels nearby, laying out cloths and tinctures. Maybe twelve. Maybe sixteen. His voice hasn’t cracked yet, but his hands move like someone who’s sutured wounds that mattered.

His shirt says FUCK COMPLIANCE in hand-scrawled ink.

He guides an old woman down beside him. Her eyes are milky with time. She’s muttering.

“No name. No name. No name left. Took it. Took it.”

He holds her hand.

“It’s okay. We don’t need them to give it back.”

She twitches. Blinks.

“I was… I was…”

“You were Magdalene,” he says. “I read your name on the wall.”

Her breathing slows. Just a little.

“Magdalene,” she repeats.

He nods. Smiles without teeth. “Yeah. That’s yours again.”

I don’t breathe for a second. Not because it’s holy. Because it’s unfamiliar.

No scans. No injections. Just listening. Just witnessing.

He looks over at me. “You got pain?”

“Too much.”

“You want to keep it?”

“No.”

He nods. “Then we’ll start with your hands.”

He wraps my fingers in soaked linen. The cold bites, but it helps. Kat was right—my grip’s been off. Maybe from the sword. Maybe from holding on too long.

He says nothing else. Neither do I.

Until I ask: “What do you do when someone hurts someone else?”

The older woman—not Magdalene—glances over. “You mean justice?”

I nod.

She shrugs. “We ask what broke in them first.”

“And if they don’t answer?”

“Then we keep asking. Loud enough that the whole camp hears.”

“And punishment?”

“If it comes to that,” she says, “it’s not about making them pay. It’s about making sure it doesn’t happen again.”

She picks up a root with silver veins. Begins to grind.

“We’re not perfect,” she adds. “But we don’t burn people for sins they never got to name.”



####

Later, near the main firepit, Kat stands among the elders.

She doesn’t speak loud. Doesn’t raise her arms. Just listens. Laughs once. Nods. Offers a solution to something about water access and wasted space. It’s not a command. It’s an offering.

They accept it. Not because she demanded it. Because she’s one of them. A voice, not a verdict.

Bambi watches from beside me. Arms crossed. Jaw tight.

“You still don’t get it, do you?”

I glance over. “Get what?”

He nods toward her. “She doesn’t lead because she wants to. She leads because she listens.”

I want to believe that. I do.

“She was gone for years,” he says. “And not a damn thing fell apart. But now she’s back, and it all hums better. Like the canyon missed her rhythm.”

She catches us looking. Doesn’t wave. Just tilts her chin in that way she does when she’s trying not to smile.

“She’s home,” Bambi says.

And for a second, so am I.



####

Night comes soft, but I can’t follow it.

Kat sleeps with one arm over her face, the other thrown across my chest like she’s trying to hold down the sky. Her breath is steady. Warm. Real.

I stare at the sword.

It leans against the wall. Still silent. But not still. Not really.

It hums—just enough to be noticed. Not loud. Not urgent. Just… present. Like a dog waiting at the door.

I should throw it. Bury it. Break it. But it’s part of me. Or maybe I’m part of it.

Sleep won’t come. Not after a day like that. Not after joy like that.

The laughter. The stories. The meals. It was too much. Too bright. Too raw.

Joy is louder than trauma. Harder to carry. There’s no training for it. No handbook. Just hope.

Kat shifts beside me. Her hand finds mine in the dark.

“You okay?”

“No.”

She pulls herself closer. Head on my chest. Voice low.

“This is the hard part,” she whispers. “Believin’ we’re allowed to breathe.”

I don’t answer.

Not because I disagree.

But because I do.



####

The Child sits across, legs folded. Watching. Always watching.

“You smiled,” he says.

“I think I did.”

“Did you believe it was real?”

“I wanted to.”

“And now?”

I look down. The cracks have started to spread.

“Now I know it was,” I say. “That’s why it hurt so much to lose.”

The Child doesn’t move. Doesn’t blink.

The void flickers. Not like lights. Like memory. Like something being erased line by line.

The sword doesn’t hum.

We sit in the flicker.

Not afraid.

Not ready.

Just... present.
